abstract:
This documentary collection provides a comprehensive comparative analysis of the Soviet occupation in Romania, Hungary, and Austria between 1944 and 1949. Utilizing recently declassified archival materials from all three nations and the Russian Federation, the volume examines the multifaceted nature of Soviet imperial expansion and the simultaneous process of Sovietization in East-Central Europe. The study focuses on five critical dimensions: the evolution of Soviet foreign policy in the early Cold War, the systematic economic exploitation of occupied territories through reparations and joint-stock companies, the administrative and repressive roles of the Red Army and NKVD, the surveillance activities of Soviet intelligence services, and the treatment and repatriation of prisoners of war. 

By juxtaposing the experiences of these three countries, the research highlights how Stalinist strategies varied from the unilateral political transformation seen in Romania and Hungary to the unique four-power administration that ultimately preserved Austrian sovereignty. The evidence suggests that Soviet penetration was driven more by long-term strategic and economic goals—such as securing a permanent defensive cordon and extracting natural resources—than as a direct reaction to Western policies like the Marshall Plan. Ultimately, this work offers new insights into the mechanics of Soviet hegemony and the varying regional responses to the imposition of the Eastern Bloc system.
